statement.markdown: Given a non-negative integer k, write a function that represents it as a sum of 1 (1 <= i <= n) such that each i appears at most once in the sum. If there is a way to represent k as a sum, return true, else return false. For example, 12 = 11 + 0 + 1 = 2 + 2 + 2 + 6, so return true. 13 can be represented as 9 + 4 = 13, but 3 + 4 + 5 + 1 = 13 cannot be represented. Return false in this case.
statement.inputFormat: Input is a non-negative integer k
statement.outputFormat: Return true if k can be represented as a sum of 1..n, else return false

hints.text: Consider using a dynamic programming approach to solve this problem

editorial.title: Dynamic Programming for Sum of 1..n
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by using dynamic programming to find the minimum number of elements required to represent the given number.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach is to try all possible combinations of numbers from 1 to n.
editorial.betterSolution: The better solution is to use dynamic programming to find the minimum number of elements required to represent the given number.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a boolean array dp where dp[i] is true if i can be represented as a sum of 1..n. The final answer is dp[k].
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the fact that if i can be represented as a sum of 1..n, then i can be represented as a sum of 1..n-1 and 1.
editorial.dryRun: Consider the example k = 12. The dry run will be dp = [false, false, false, false, false, false, false, false, false, false, false, true].
commonMistakes: Using a recursive approach instead of dynamic programming
